timeline_link.text: See on Timeline — Call of Jeremiah

sections.header: Verses 1–9 — "Even if Moses and Samuel Stood Before Me"

heb.paragraph: God says he will "send away" (šillaḥ) the people from his presence (v.1). The same verb is used for divorce (Deut 24:1) and for sending away Hagar (Gen 21:14). The covenant relationship is being formally terminated — God is divorcing his people.

refs.note: Moses' successful intercession after the golden calf — the paradigmatic prayer of prophetic mediation that is now declared insufficient.
Samuel's intercession at Mizpah — another model of effective prophetic prayer now declared powerless.
Ezekiel makes the same point with different names: "Even if Noah, Daniel, and Job were in it, they could save only themselves."

notes.note: Moses and Samuel were the two greatest intercessors in Israel's history. Saying that even their prayers would fail is the ultimate statement of irrevocable judgment.
Four destinations: death, sword, starvation, captivity. There is no escape route — every exit leads to destruction.

Four destroyers and one cause: "because of what Manasseh son of Hezekiah king of Judah did in Jerusalem." Manasseh's sins — child sacrifice, divination, shedding innocent blood — have accumulated beyond remedy.

Calvin is struck by the extremity: "When God declares that even the prayers of Moses and Samuel cannot avail, he announces that the door of mercy is shut — not because prayer is powerless, but because the sentence is irreversible."
The phrase "stand before me" (ʿāmad lĕpānay) is technical language for intercessory prayer — the posture of the mediator before the divine throne.

Manasseh's 55-year reign (697–642 BC) was the longest in Judean history and the most apostate. 2 Kings 21 catalogs his offenses; 2 Kings 23:26 confirms that God did not turn from his anger because of Manasseh.

Lundbom notes that invoking Moses and Samuel simultaneously invokes two pivotal moments of near-destruction: the golden calf and the Philistine crisis. Both times intercession saved Israel. Now even these paradigm-shattering precedents are declared insufficient.

Brueggemann calls this "the closure of prophetic mediation." The intercessory tradition — Israel's primary mechanism for averting divine wrath — is declared bankrupt. What remains is not negotiation but endurance.

hist.context: The chapter opens with one of the most severe declarations in prophetic literature: "Even if Moses and Samuel were to stand before me, my heart would not go out to this people" (v.1). Moses and Samuel were Israel's greatest intercessors (Exod 32:11–14; 1 Sam 7:5–9). If even their prayers would be ineffective, all intercession is closed. The "four kinds of destroyers" (v.3) — sword, dogs, birds, beasts — represent total annihilation: death, desecration, exposure, consumption. Manasseh's sins (v.4, cf. 2 Kgs 21) have sealed Judah's fate.

sections.header: Verses 10–21 — "Your Words Were My Joy and My Heart's Delight"

heb.paragraph: Jeremiah testifies: "When your words (dĕbāreykā) came, I ate them; they were my joy and my heart's delight" (v.16). The metaphor of eating God's word combines Ezekiel 3:1–3 (eating the scroll) with the psalmist's "sweeter than honey" (Ps 119:103). But the delight has become a burden: the word that brought joy now brings isolation, suffering, and rejection.

refs.note: Ezekiel eats the scroll that is "sweet as honey" — the same metaphor of consuming God's word.
John eats the little scroll: "sweet in my mouth but sour in my stomach." The pattern — delightful reception, painful proclamation — runs from Jeremiah to Revelation.
The recommission (v.20) repeats the language of chapter 1: "I will make you a wall of bronze." The original call is renewed after the crisis.

notes.note: "Alas, my mother, that you gave me birth!" — the prophet's birth-curse anticipates the more extreme version in 20:14–18. The prophetic vocation has become unbearable.

"I ate your words and they were my joy" — this is the most intimate description of prophetic calling in the OT. God's word is not merely heard or spoken but consumed — it becomes part of the prophet's very being.

Jeremiah sat alone because God's hand was upon him, filled with indignation. Then the devastating accusation: God is like "a deceptive brook, a spring that fails." The prophet accuses God of unreliability.

God's response is not comfort but recommission: "If you repent, I will restore you." The prophet must undergo the same transformation he demands of others. The promise: "I will make you a wall of bronze" — the chapter 1 language renewed.

Calvin celebrates this verse as "the testimony of every true minister: God's word is first delight before it becomes duty. Without the initial sweetness, the subsequent bitterness would be insupportable."
Calvin reads God's call to repentance as evidence that even prophets are sinners: "Jeremiah needed to extract the precious from the worthless in his own heart before he could do so for others."
The phrase "I am called by your name" (niqrāʾ šimkā ʿālay) indicates ownership — Jeremiah belongs to God as God's named possession. The same formula appears in the covenant with Israel (Deut 28:10).
The "deceptive brook" (ʾakzāb) is a wadi that flows in winter but dries up in summer when water is most needed. The metaphor accuses God of being present in prosperity but absent in crisis.

Lundbom classifies this as the third and most intense confession. The progression — birth curse (v.10), word-delight (v.16), isolation (v.17), accusation (v.18), recommission (vv.19–21) — traces the full arc of prophetic crisis and renewal.

Brueggemann identifies the "deceptive brook" accusation as the most daring speech in the book. The prophet accuses God of unreliability — and God responds not with rebuke but with conditional restoration. The relationship survives the accusation because it is honest.

hist.context: Verses 10–21 constitute Jeremiah's third and most intense "confession." The prophet curses the day of his birth (v.10, anticipating 20:14–18), complains of loneliness (v.17), accuses God of being "a deceptive brook" (v.18), and receives a conditional recommission: "If you repent, I will restore you, and you will stand before me" (v.19). The prophet who called others to repent must himself repent. God's response is not comfort but renewal of commission — with the same "fortified wall" language from chapter 1.

rows.label: vv.1–4
rows.text: Even Moses and Samuel Cannot Intercede

rows.label: vv.15–18
rows.text: "Your Words Were My Joy" / Deceptive Brook

lit.note: Chapter 15 pairs the most extreme judgment oracle (vv.1–9) with the most intimate prophetic confession (vv.10–21). The Moses/Samuel reference closes intercessory prayer; the recommission (vv.19–21) reopens prophetic ministry on renewed terms.

themes.note: Judgment peaks at 10 — Moses and Samuel cannot intercede. Prophecy at 9 reflects the confession and recommission. Faith at 6: "your words were my joy."

hebtext.note: God says he will "send away" (šillaḥ) the people from his presence (v.1). The same verb is used for divorce (Deut 24:1) and for sending away Hagar (Gen 21:14). The covenant relationship is being formally terminated — God is divorcing his people.

Jeremiah testifies: "When your words (dĕbāreykā) came, I ate them; they were my joy and my heart's delight" (v.16). The metaphor of eating God's word combines Ezekiel 3:1–3 (eating the scroll) with the psalmist's "sweeter than honey" (Ps 119:103). But the delight has become a burden: the word that brought joy now brings isolation, suffering, and rejection.

ppl.name: Jeremiah
ppl.text: Verses 10–21 constitute Jeremiah's third and most intense "confession…

ppl.name: Moses
ppl.text: Moses stands at the centre of the Pentateuch’s narrative — the one man who spoke with God face to face (Exod 33:11; Num 12:8) and mediated the Sinai covenant to Israel. In Leviticus he receives the priestly legislation; in Numbers he leads, intercedes, and ultimately fails at Meribah (Num 20). The writer of Hebrews calls him "faithful in all God’s house" (Heb 3:5) while distinguishing him from Christ, the Son over the house.

trans.title: Comparing translations in God's Irrevocable Judgment

translations.text: And if they ask you, ‘Where shall we go?’ tell them, ‘This is what the Lord says: “ ‘Those destined for death, to death; those for the sword, to the sword; those for starvation, to starvation; those for captivity, to captivity.’

And when they ask you, ‘Where shall we go?’ you shall say to them, ‘Thus says the LORD: “‘Those who are for pestilence, to pestilence, and those who are for the sword, to the sword; those who are for famine, to famine, and those who are for captivity, to captivity.’
You have rejected me,” declares the Lord. “You keep on backsliding. So I will reach out and destroy you; I am tired of holding back.
You have rejected me, declares the LORD; you keep going backward, so I have stretched out my hand against you and destroyed you— I am weary of relenting.

rec.title: John MacArthur
rec.quote: "Moses and Samuel were the two greatest intercessors in Israel's history. Saying that even their prayers would fail is the ultimate statement of irrevocable judgment."

rec.quote: "Calvin is struck by the extremity: "When God declares that even the prayers of Moses and Samuel cannot avail, he announces that the door of mercy is shut — not because prayer is powerless, but because…"

rec.quote: "The phrase "stand before me" (ʿāmad lĕpānay) is technical language for intercessory prayer — the posture of the mediator before the divine throne."
